narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a respectable 36 on the LSAT and planning to attend full-time. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated your probability of failing at 48%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. The primary reason for this decision was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which stood out well below the typical student's average of around 5.5 and had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure. This low ranking signaled to the model a notable dip in performance later in your academic path compared to peers. On a more positive note, your LSAT score, close to the dataset average of 36.7, strongly pulled the prediction back toward passing success. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, though below the average of 3.21, also contributed somewhat positively by not dragging the odds down as much as it might have for others. Factors like your family income bracket and full-time status offered minor boosts toward a passing prediction, aligning with common profiles among successful bar takers. Your Year 1 decile of 4 was roughly average and had little sway overall. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your profile showed strengths in standardized testing and background but was held back most by that Year 3 performance, which deviated sharply from the norm. While this outcome is disappointing, it highlights clear areas for growth. We encourage you to consider retaking courses or seeking academic support to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps exploring part-time options or GPA improvement programs, and reapplying once you've strengthened these key areas—many students turn predictions around with targeted effort. Your potential is evident, and with focus, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
